metric_value: Face-to-face structured RAMP interview guide
notes: Designed for in-person executive interviews as part of the Maxtor RAMP validation project with approximately 5 enterprise companies.

metric_value: Internal DAS / External DAS / SAN / NAS — four-way breakout
notes: The four-way architecture taxonomy (internal DAS / external DAS / SAN / NAS) was the dominant enterprise categorization of 2003. This became the canonical segmentation used across all subsequent storage market research.

metric_value: SCSI / Fibre Channel / ATA — three technology types; size (GB) and rotational speed
notes: Assumption embedded in Q7: the three-way disk technology split (SCSI / FC / ATA) reflects 2003 market reality. Notably ATA is included as a distinct category, not an afterthought — indicating Aberdeen's hypothesis that ATA had already penetrated some enterprise environments.

metric_value: Performance bottleneck sources: CPU / network / storage — three-way choice
notes: Q8 assumes bottlenecks occur in one of three loci: CPU, network, or storage. This framework anticipated the storage I/O bottleneck debates of 2005-2010 as virtualization concentrated workloads, and the network bottleneck that drove 10GbE and then 40/100GbE adoption.

metric_value: Not all enterprises have formal SLAs; probe for informal vs. formal availability/performance criteria
notes: Q9 probes whether availability/performance requirements are informal or SLA-formalized. This assumption that informal practices were common in 2003 proved correct; storage SLA discipline became a major driver of ITIL adoption and later cloud service tier definitions.

metric_value: Number of tape automation products / number of drives / number of slots
notes: Q10 reveals the design assumption that all large enterprise sites had tape automation. This was accurate for enterprises with >1 TB in 2003 and reflects the tape/disk divide that the midline tier was designed to bridge.

metric_value: Running out of backup window or jobs failing to complete — explicitly surfaced as known issue
notes: Q11 treats backup window overflow as a known, anticipated problem rather than asking if it exists. This embedded assumption proved correct and became a primary driver of continuous data protection (CDP), deduplication, and later backup-to-disk then backup-to-cloud adoption.

metric_value: Retention criteria: age / transaction closure / regulatory — open-ended
notes: Q14 probes retention policy logic. The framing of "age" and "transaction closure" as retention triggers directly maps to what became ILM policy engines (EMC Documentum ILM, IBM Tivoli Storage Manager policies, and later AWS S3 lifecycle rules).

metric_value: On-disk copies / on-site tape copies / offsite DR tape copies — three-location model
notes: Q15's three-location topology (local disk / on-site tape / offsite DR tape) was the standard enterprise BC/DR model in 2003. This directly evolved into the 3-2-1 backup rule and then cloud-based offsite replication that replaced offsite tape shipping by 2015.

metric_value: Probes whether any storage tier exists BETWEEN high-performance disk and tape
notes: Q16 is the pivotal question of the entire instrument: it directly tests whether respondents have already invented their own midline tier. The framing assumes only two tiers existed (performance disk + tape) and asks if respondents had bridged the gap — a prescient probe of the market vacuum MaXLine aimed to fill.

metric_value: Eight application types across Database/File categories with I/O signatures
notes: The eight-category application classification (traditional OLTP / contemporary OLTP / BI / web app / personal productivity / interactive design / unique large files / large file distribution) with I/O read/write signatures is a rigorous analytical framework. It maps directly to the workload classifications used in modern all-flash array sizing, object storage tier selection, and cloud storage class recommendations.

metric_value: Update-intensive (both reads and writes; focus on writes)
notes: Traditional OLTP characterized as write-dominant. This classification remained accurate and underpins storage array QoS design through the present day.

metric_value: Mixed reads and writes — updating for transactions but also random reads (SAP R/3 cited)
notes: SAP R/3 explicitly cited as the exemplar of mixed-I/O OLTP. This anticipates the later discovery that ERP systems running on-premises required tiered storage for different data ages — a key driver of SAP HANA's in-memory design and SAP's push to move historical data to cheaper storage.

metric_value: Query-intensive (primarily sequential reads)
notes: BI/DW characterized as sequential-read-dominant. This correctly anticipated that ATA/SATA disks — optimized for capacity over random IOPS — would be acceptable for BI workloads, a prediction confirmed by the widespread SATA-backed DW appliances of 2006-2012 (Netezza, Teradata Active EDW, etc.).

metric_value: Write-once / read-once-to-many times — personal productivity category
notes: E-mail classified as write-once/read-few — the canonical midline storage candidate. This hypothesis was confirmed: enterprise email archiving to lower-cost SATA-backed arrays became a standard pattern by 2007 (EMC Centera, Iron Mountain digital, and later cloud email archiving).

metric_value: Higher capacity disks with slightly lesser performance and availability — option for data migration
notes: Per-application Q17 option (a) tests acceptance of the core MaXLine value proposition: more capacity / lower cost / slightly lower performance. This binary choice design (similar vs. less-capable) directly measured the performance-cost tradeoff sensitivity that determined ATA's enterprise viability.

metric_name: Data migration benefit framing — manageability vs. cost
metric_value: Q18 probes whether benefit is manageability-focused or cost-focused
notes: Q18's manageability-vs.-cost probe anticipated that the midline story was not purely economic — storage admin labor reduction was equally important. This proved correct: the managed storage / tiered storage management software market (Symantec Storage Foundation, EMC ILM suite) grew rapidly on the manageability argument.

metric_value: Q7 probes whether arrays are shared across applications — and WHY if not
notes: The assumption that shared arrays were common but not universal correctly reflected 2003 practice. Application-dedicated storage silos were a major cost and management inefficiency that SAN virtualization (EMC Invista, IBM SVC, HDS USP-V) and later software-defined storage aimed to eliminate.

metric_value: "Data that spins around" — closed transactions / old e-mail as migration candidates; estimated % of total
notes: The phrase "data that spins around" to describe inactive-but-online data directly tests the ILM concept. The explicit request for percentage estimate was designed to size the addressable market for midline storage — a methodology that became standard in tiered storage market sizing.

metric_value: Must be ≥10% of total storage OR ≥0.5 TB to qualify for third application slot
notes: The explicit 10% / 0.5 TB threshold for a third application reflects the 2003 enterprise storage scale reality (typical enterprises managed 5-20 TB). By 2010 a 0.5 TB threshold would be trivially small; this threshold implicitly assumed total storage in the single-digit TB range.

metric_value: Term ""nearline storage"" tested for awareness
notes: Testing whether respondents knew "nearline" reveals the hypothesis that the concept existed but the term was not yet standardized in 2003. This proved correct: nearline became an industry-standard tier designation by 2006-2008, appearing in SNIA standards and vendor product names (Seagate Nearline, Hitachi Nearline).

metric_value: Term ""active archiving"" tested for awareness
notes: Active archiving as a term was still nascent in 2003. It became a major marketing category by 2006-2010, used by EMC (Centera), HP StorageWorks, and Iron Mountain. The term's inclusion in the guide shows Aberdeen anticipated the need for a new vocabulary for the midline use case.

metric_value: Term ""mezzanine storage"" tested for awareness
notes: Mezzanine storage was an alternative term for the midline concept. It did NOT become the winning vocabulary — nearline and midline won. This terminology test correctly identified that the market lacked consensus on naming.

metric_name: Disk capacity growth vs. speed divergence
metric_value: Disk capacity increasing faster than rotational speed — acknowledged as a known tension
notes: The capacity-speed divergence question directly acknowledges the Kryder's Law vs. IOPS problem that became the central storage architecture challenge of 2005-2015, ultimately resolved by flash/SSD for performance and large-capacity SATA for bulk. The framing was prescient.

metric_value: [DEFERRED]
notes: Capacity-performance divergence accelerated through 2005-2015 as predicted; resolved by flash for performance tier and high-density SATA/SAS for capacity tier. NVMe emerging by 2015 for extreme performance. Midline SATA proved the winner for the "good enough" enterprise tier.

metric_value: CIO reports to: CFO / President-CEO / Other
notes: Probing CIO reporting line was a proxy for IT budget authority and storage investment decision-making power. In 2003, CIO-to-CFO reporting was growing (IT cost focus); by 2010 CIO-to-CEO reporting became more common as IT became strategic. The question anticipated IT governance debates of the 2000s.

metric_value: Interview covers primary DB app + primary file app + optional third app — three-application coverage design
notes: The three-application coverage design (primary DB / primary file / third app) was designed to capture at least 80-90% of enterprise storage by targeting the two largest consumers. This approach implicitly acknowledges storage concentration in large applications.

metric_value: 12-month storage growth projection requested — both total and per-application
notes: Probing 12-month growth expectations in 2003 would have revealed the storage explosion driven by email retention, regulatory compliance (Sarbanes-Oxley 2002, HIPAA), and unstructured data growth — all of which proved to be structural demand drivers for midline storage.

metric_value: Maximum acceptable % full for disk storage — probed as management policy
notes: Probing maximum utilization threshold (typically 70-80% in practice) quantifies the "dark storage" or over-provisioning that was endemic in 2003. This over-provisioning was a key economic argument for midline storage: if you could fill a midline disk to 90% rather than leaving 30% headroom on expensive FC arrays, the savings multiplied.

metric_value: OS breakout: IBM mainframe / Unix / Windows / Other — matched to storage architecture and capacity
notes: The OS-to-storage-architecture mapping question reveals the assumption that different OSes would have different storage architecture preferences (mainframe → internal/ESCON; Unix → SAN; Windows → DAS/NAS). This assumption proved largely correct and explains why ATA/SATA gained fastest in Windows environments.
